name: Ebro Reservoir Loop
name_original: Embalse del Ebro — circular desde Arija
description: Cycle 36.9 miles around Embalse del Ebro from Arija, enjoying moderate road cycling through unspoilt scenery and historical landmarks.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Arija traverse a landscape characterized by the Ebro Reservoir, offering varied terrain from lakeside paths to more challenging ascents. The region features a mix of open countryside and routes that can lead through forested areas and past natural rock formations. Road cyclists can expect routes that navigate both flat sections and significant elevation gains, providing diverse experiences. Best road cycling routes around Arija The most popular road cycling route is Ebro Reservoir Loop, a 36.9 miles (59.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 34 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers scenic views around the reservoir. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Source of the Ebro River – Aurora Fountain loop from Arija, a difficult 70.9 miles (114.1 km) path. This route features substantial climbs and diverse natural scenery, including the source of the Ebro River. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto de Carrales – Orbaneja del Castillo loop from Arija, a 52.3 miles (84.2 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain and past notable villages, often completed in about 3 hours 46 minutes. Road cycling around Arija is defined by reservoir views, significant climbs, and routes through varied natural landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to challenging long-distance rides. The routes in Arija are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from 20 reviews. More than 400 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Arija's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Arija?
A: Road cycling routes around Arija offer a diverse landscape, characterized by the Ebro Reservoir. You'll find a mix of open countryside, forested areas, and natural rock formations. Routes vary from flat sections along the lake to significant elevation gains, providing a varied experience for cyclists.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Arija?
A: Yes, Arija offers routes suitable for beginners. While many routes feature significant climbs, there are options with less challenging profiles. For instance, the region has at least one easy-rated route, and several moderate options like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057553235/ebro-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ebro Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 36.9 miles (59.4 km) with moderate elevation, can be a good starting point.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes in the Arija area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Arija offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31486346" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Ebro River – Aurora Fountain loop from Arija&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 70.9 miles (114.1 km) ride with substantial climbs. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40324451" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Natural Rock Arch of Puentedey – Puentedey loop from Arija&lt;/a&gt;, covering 70.3 miles (113.2 km) with significant elevation.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Arija?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Arija are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Popular circular routes include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057553235/ebro-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ebro Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20162270" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Carrales – Orbaneja del Castillo loop from Arija&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of scenic viewpoints or attractions can I find along road cycling routes in Arija?
A: Road cycling routes in Arija often feature scenic views of the Ebro Reservoir and the surrounding natural landscapes. You can also encounter interesting landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5084696" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fountain and Old Bridge at Embalse del Ebro&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable points of interest in the wider area include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5346281" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hijedo Cabin&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3300130" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pyramid of the Italians&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Arija?
A: The road cycling routes in Arija are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.4 stars from 20 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the scenic views around the Ebro Reservoir, and the options available for different ability levels, from moderate loops to challenging long-distance rides.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Arija region?
A: There are over 15 distinct road cycling routes available around Arija, offering a wide range of distances and difficulty levels. This includes a mix of easy, moderate, and difficult options to suit various preferences.

Q: Are there any routes that pass through notable villages or historical sites?
A: Yes, some routes will take you through charming villages and past historical sites. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20162270" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Carrales – Orbaneja del Castillo loop from Arija&lt;/a&gt; leads through mountainous terrain and past notable villages. You might also pass by places like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6333376" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corconte&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in Arija?
A: The duration of road cycling routes in Arija varies significantly based on distance and difficulty. Shorter, moderate loops like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1057553235/ebro-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ebro Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in about 2 hours 34 minutes. Longer, more challenging routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31493036" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Carrales – Puente de Piedra de Polientes loop from Arija&lt;/a&gt;, can take upwards of 4 hours 30 minutes.

Q: What are the elevation gains like on road cycling routes around Arija?
A: Road cycling in Arija features diverse elevation profiles. While some routes have relatively moderate climbs, many include significant ascents. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31486346" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Ebro River – Aurora Fountain loop from Arija&lt;/a&gt; involves over 1,300 meters of elevation gain, making it a challenging ride for those looking for substantial climbs.

Q: Is it possible to find routes that explore the source of the Ebro River?
A: Yes, there is a specific route that takes you to the source of the Ebro River. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31486346" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Ebro River – Aurora Fountain loop from Arija&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 70.9 miles (114.1 km) path that features substantial climbs and diverse natural scenery, including the source of the Ebro River itself.
tips: The Tower of the Church of San Roque, popularly known as the Cathedral of the Fish, rises above the waters of the Ebro reservoir. With the construction of the reservoir, between 1921 and 1945, towns in the region, such as Medianedo, La Magdalena and Quintanilla, disappeared under its waters. A must visit for everyone visiting the area - the place is super nice and quiet.
In "La Llanuca" are the large houses of the ancient city of Juliobriga from Roman times, about 1,200 m² and comparable to current mansions. These belonged to the upper class of the city.
Nice road bike route bordering the Ebro reservoir, Concorte, Cantabria.
Road that runs along the edge of the Ebro Reservoir itself and offers beautiful views of the north of the swamp.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Embalse del Ebro — circular desde Arija route for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is considered moderate for road cycling. It covers approximately 59.4 kilometers with about 500 meters of elevation gain. While primarily on well-paved surfaces, it requires a good fitness level due to various climbs and descents. It's generally suitable for experienced road cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features well-paved roads, making it ideal for a road bike. You'll encounter quiet country roads with varied gradients, including both descents and climbs. The scenery is diverse, ranging from lakeside stretches to meadows and oak forests.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Embalse del Ebro — circular desde Arija route?
faqs.answer: The route is circular and starts from Arija. You should be able to find suitable parking within or near the village of Arija to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What are the main sights and natural features along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll cycle along the beautiful Embalse del Ebro, which resembles a large lake, offering stunning views. Key highlights include the Tower of the Church of San Roque (The Cathedral of the Fish), the La Llanuca Roman Ruins, and the Viewpoint of the Ebro Reservoir. You'll also pass the Fountain and Old Bridge at Embalse del Ebro. Keep an eye out for the striking submerged church tower of Villanueva de las Rozas, which often emerges from the water.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The area is known for vibrant wildflowers, particularly in spring, making it a beautiful time to visit. Generally, spring, summer, and early autumn offer pleasant cycling conditions. Always check local weather forecasts before you go.

faqs.question: Is the Embalse del Ebro — circular desde Arija route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is on public roads, it's generally not recommended to bring dogs along on a road cycling route of this length and type, especially given the traffic and speed involved. If you do bring a dog, ensure they are safely secured and that you comply with all local regulations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required for cycling around Embalse del Ebro?
faqs.answer: The Embalse del Ebro area is part of the Zona de Especial Protección para las Aves Embalse del Ebro (Special Protection Area for Birds). While there are no specific entrance fees for cycling on the public roads, it's important to respect wildlife and adhere to any environmental regulations, especially regarding noise and staying on marked paths to protect the bird habitats.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that overlap with the Camino Olvidado (Bimón-Matamorisca), Sendero Histórico: Cantabria, Camino Olvidado (Villasante-Bimón), Transcantábrica, Asturian Way, and the Camino natural de Ebro - Cantabria.

faqs.question: Is this a suitable route for families with children?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty, length of nearly 60 km, and elevation gain, this specific route is generally more suitable for experienced adult cyclists. Families with younger children or those new to road cycling might find it too demanding. Shorter, flatter sections around the reservoir might be more appropriate for a family outing.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or find accommodation near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you'll find villages like Arija along the route and in the surrounding area. These often offer local cafes, restaurants, and various accommodation options, providing opportunities to refuel or stay overnight.

faqs.question: What should I wear and bring for road cycling this route?
faqs.answer: For this moderate road cycling route, wear appropriate cycling attire, including a helmet, padded shorts, and layers suitable for the weather. Bring plenty of water, snacks, a repair kit for your bike, a fully charged phone, and sun protection. Given the varied terrain, consider a GPS device or a map for navigation.
